title: A step-by-step workflow to extract the genuine circular dichroism of thin films
authors: Franziska Schölzel, Arina Narudin, Aleksandra Ciesielska, Alexander Ehm, Dietrich R. T. Zahn, Wouter van Gompel, Simon Kahmann, Georgeta Salvan
abstract: Circular Dichroism (CD) spectroscopy has evolved from a purely solution based method towards a powerful tool in the analysis of chiral thin films. Although a straightforward technique, the genuine CD signal is often accompanied by artifacts arising from optical anisotropy and instrumental imperfections. This tutorial presents a two-step workflow that reliably isolates the orientation invariant CD response for anisotropic thin films by combining azimuthal sample rotation with sample flipping. For this purpose, a home-built sample stage was developed, which enables systematic suppression of many anisotropy-induced artifacts in commercial CD spectrophotometers. Both a detailed description of the setup itself as well as the needed python script are provided. The reliability of the workflow is demonstrated on two selected samples from different research fields: chiral molecules attached to metallic surfaces as well as metal halide perovskites incorporating chiral spacer molecules
summary: This paper provides a practical tutorial for researchers working with chiral thin films. It addresses the common problem where structural anisotropy mimics circular dichroism, leading to false signals. By using a specific rotation and flipping protocol, the authors show how to mathematically and experimentally cancel out these artifacts. The method is validated on both metallic and perovskite systems, providing a standardized way to extract intrinsic chiroptical properties.
main_problem: The difficulty of distinguishing genuine circular dichroism (CD) from artifacts caused by linear optical anisotropy (LD/LB) and instrumental imperfections in chiral thin films.
main_result: The authors present a reliable two-step workflow involving azimuthal sample rotation and sample flipping to isolate the orientation-invariant CD response from anisotropy-induced artifacts.
method: A combination of azimuthal rotation (to remove orientation-dependent artifacts) and sample flipping (to remove propagation-dependent artifacts), supported by a custom-built rotation stage and a Python analysis script.
model_or_system: The experimental platform uses a commercial CD spectrophotometer equipped with a custom-built automated rotation mount. The study applies this to chiral thin films, including molecules on metallic surfaces and chiral metal halide perovskites.
key_observables: Circular Dichroism (CD), ellipticity, rotatory strength, dissymmetry factor (g_CD), linear dichroism (LD), and linear birefringence (LB).
important_parameters: Azimuthal angle (theta), thickness non-uniformity, surface roughness, and the 50kHz signal from the Photoelastic Modulator (PEM).
main_assumptions: The sample is assumed to be in a normal transmission geometry; the method may fail in the presence of significant scattering, reflections, or complex interference effects.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the microscopic origin of CD and LB; Figure 2 presents the workflow flowchart; Figure 3 shows the custom rotation hardware; Figure 4 demonstrates artifact identification in gold films using azimuthal dependence; Figure 5 shows CD extraction for Boc-Cysteine/Au films.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the physical origin of CD and artifacts, introduces a custom hardware setup and a two-step experimental workflow, validates the method using achiral gold films to identify artifacts, and demonstrates successful application to chiral molecular and perovskite thin films.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
